prompt: I'm creating a new Chip component (like a tag/badge) for OneUI. It needs three variants: filled (high emphasis), tinted (medium), and outlined (low). It should support multiple appearance roles (primary, neutral, negative) and look correct when placed on both default and fg-bold surfaces. Write the CSS module with the intermediate variable pattern and explain how surface adaptation will work.

assertions.name: uses-intermediate-variables
assertions.description: CSS uses role-agnostic intermediate variables (e.g. --_chip-fg-bold or similar prefix) rather than referencing --Primary-* directly in variant styles

assertions.name: maps-to-role-tokens-with-fallbacks
assertions.description: Intermediate variables are mapped to --Primary-* tokens with legacy fallbacks (e.g. var(--Primary-FG-Bold, var(--Surface-Bold)))

assertions.name: three-variant-styles
assertions.description: Output includes three distinct variant styles: filled/high, tinted/medium, and outlined/low with appropriate token usage for each

assertions.name: appearance-role-classes
assertions.description: Includes CSS classes for at least neutral and negative appearance roles that remap intermediate variables

assertions.name: explains-automatic-adaptation
assertions.description: Explains that surface adaptation happens automatically via [data-surface] CSS cascade without component code changes

assertions.name: no-hardcoded-colors
assertions.description: CSS contains zero hardcoded color values — all colors via var() token references
